head: 44c1476

benchmarks_run_locally.interpretation: At 10k rps, the matcher path alone allocates ~7.3 MB/s and ~390k allocs/s. CPU cost is ~1.5% of one core - tolerable, but allocation pressure is real and the per-call cost is dominated by re-parsing the same static patterns on every request.

evidence.comparison_vs_legacy: Legacy getFailsafeExecutor: 1 WildcardMatch per executor (skipped for fe.method == '*'). New: 1-2 WildcardMatch per matcher per executor (network skipped if empty). For converted-from-legacy configs (1 matcher per executor) the per-executor cost is similar. For users adopting multi-matcher rules (the new feature), cost grows linearly with N matchers - linear is correct but uncached parsing makes it worse than necessary.
evidence.convertLegacyMatchers_runtime: Verified: invoked only from CachePolicyConfig.SetDefaults (NewCachePolicy at startup) and FailsafeConfig.Normalize (FailsafeConfig.SetDefaults at startup). NOT per-request. No bug here.

findings.title: WildcardMatch tokenizes + parses + builds closures on every request - this is the gating concern from review #5/#18
findings.evidence: Benchmark on the actual code: WildcardMatch allocates 56-104 bytes and 3-5 heap objects per call (token slice, parser struct, parsePrimary closure capturing the pattern string). At 10k rps with a typical request flowing through ~3-4 failsafe matchers + ~5 cache policies (each with 2 WildcardMatch invocations on network + method patterns), this is ~14-18 WildcardMatch calls per request -> 140-180k allocs/s and ~10-18 MB/s of GC pressure originating from this single path. The patterns are STATIC after config load (a handful of strings: "*", "eth_*", "eth_getBlockByNumber", maybe a network glob). Re-parsing them per request is pure waste.
findings.impact: This is the *exact* concern Aram raised in review #5 ("creating a new instance of an object happens 5k/sec, I was thinking of precompiling the matcher rules") and review #18 ("some tests are needed with a load of like 8k-10k rps"). The author concluded precompilation was "not really possible" - it absolutely is. At Aram's stated 8-10k rps target this becomes a measurable slice of CPU and a meaningful contributor to allocation rate / GC pause frequency.
findings.recommendation:
Pick ONE of these two cheap wins (both are valid - the first is simpler):

  (a) Cache compiled patterns globally inside common.WildcardMatch via a sync.Map[string]func(string) (bool, error). The pattern alphabet is bounded (a few dozen distinct strings across all configs); the map will plateau quickly and never grow. Hot path becomes a sync.Map.Load + closure call (~10-15 ns).

  (b) Precompile MatcherConfig.Method/Network at config-load time into a `methodMatcher func(string) bool` field on MatcherConfig (or a parallel CompiledMatcher struct held in CachePolicy / FailsafeExecutor). MatchConfig calls the compiled func directly - no parsing, no allocation. Slightly more invasive (new struct field, plumb through the SetDefaults path) but eliminates the cache-miss path entirely.

Either approach reduces Match() from 116 ns/3-allocs to ~10-20 ns/0-allocs and addresses the gating concern.
findings.also_consider: While in there: the closures returned by parseOr/parseAnd capture `prev` and `right` by reference, which means even compound patterns (e.g. "eth_*|alchemy_*") allocate a tree of closures per call. Precompiling at config time eliminates this entirely.

findings.title: matchers.Match calls req.NetworkId() twice per invocation, each acquiring r.RLock+RUnlock; the empty-string fallback is dead code
findings.evidence:
Code:
```go
networkId := ""
if req.NetworkId() != "" {
    networkId = req.NetworkId()
}
```
NormalizedRequest.NetworkId() (common/request.go:452-463) acquires r.RLock+RUnlock and returns either "n/a" (when network is nil) or the actual id - it NEVER returns empty string. So:
  - The `!= ""` branch is always true, the else-branch is unreachable.
  - The function is invoked twice per Match call, doubling the mutex round-trips.

At 10k rps with ~10 Match calls/req, this is ~200k extra unnecessary RWMutex acquisitions/sec.
findings.impact: Trivial individually but trivially fixable. Sub-percent CPU cost; small contention contribution on the request-level RWMutex which is shared with several other call sites in the same request lifecycle.
findings.recommendation: Replace lines 159-162 with `networkId := req.NetworkId()`. One-line fix, no behavior change.

findings.title: Match() always materializes JsonRpcRequest+Params even when no configured matcher uses params
findings.impact: Small CPU cost. More importantly: reading `jrpcReq.Params` here without holding `jrpcReq.RWMutex` is a data race (correctness, not performance) - `evm.NormalizeHttpJsonRpc` mutates Params under jrq.Lock() during prepareRequest, and getFailsafeExecutor runs both before and after that path during retries. Flagging here because the fix and the perf fix are the same.
findings.recommendation:
Skip the JsonRpcRequest call when no matcher in `configs` declares Params. Two ways:
  - Compute a `usesParams` bool over configs at the top of Match (cheap, O(N) over a tiny slice).
  - Better: precompute it on the FailsafeExecutor / CachePolicy at config-load time so Match doesn't have to scan.
When reading Params, hold jrpcReq.RLock to avoid the race.

findings.title: PR adds no matcher benchmarks - directly violates the gating requirement of review comment #18
findings.evidence: The matchers/ package contains exactly one file (matcher.go) - no _test.go and no _bench_test.go. The diff to networks_bench_test.go is 8 lines and only exists to keep the existing bench compiling against the new MatcherConfig schema; no new bench was added that exercises the matcher path. Review comment #18 (Oct 2025, the last review on the PR) is explicit: "We need to test and benchmark this carefully to ensure it won't impact prod as it's basically run on every single request and some tests are needed with a load of like 8k-10k rps with a normal compute instance."
findings.impact: Without checked-in benchmarks the perf claim of this PR is unverifiable in CI and there is no regression guard for future changes (e.g. when someone later does land precompilation, or when the matcher logic grows new features). The author's own gating criterion is unmet.
findings.recommendation:
Add `matchers/matcher_bench_test.go` with at minimum:
  - BenchmarkMatch_StarOnly (1 matcher, '*')
  - BenchmarkMatch_LegacyShape (1 matcher, exact method)
  - BenchmarkMatch_MultiMatcher (5 matchers, exclude+include mix)
  - BenchmarkMatch_WithParams (matcher constrained on params)
  - BenchmarkMatchConfig_NetworkAndMethod
  - BenchmarkWildcardMatch_Star / _Prefix / _Exact / _Compound
And a comparison harness (BenchmarkGetFailsafeExecutor_Old vs _New) so reviewers can verify the perf delta vs main. With the local data I gathered (1491 ns/730 B/39 allocs for the approximated request path), reviewers have a concrete baseline to compare against any future precompilation work.

non_findings.note: convertLegacyMatchers is invoked from CachePolicyConfig.SetDefaults (called once in data.NewCachePolicy at startup) and FailsafeConfig.Normalize (called from FailsafeConfig.SetDefaults at startup). Verified by searching all call sites - no per-request invocation.

non_findings.topic: Mutexes / sync.Map contention in matcher hot path
non_findings.note: matchers/ package is fully stateless. No sync.Map, sync.Mutex, or sync.RWMutex in matchers/matcher.go. The only mutex traffic comes from req.NetworkId()'s RLock (see finding perf-2).
No caches in matchers/. No retained references.

non_findings.topic: Goroutine fan-out on the matcher path
non_findings.note: No `go func(...)` in matchers/. The pre-existing async cache-set goroutine in networks.go:851 is unchanged by this PR.

non_findings.topic: Cache iteration cost in EvmJsonRpcCache.Get vs old code
non_findings.verdict: neutral to slightly cheaper for legacy-shaped configs
non_findings.note: Old MatchesForGet always called WildcardMatch on Network and Method even when those config fields were empty (would still pay the parse + closure-build cost). New MatchesForGetWithMatcher skips WildcardMatch when matcher.Network == "" or matcher.Method == "". For converted-legacy configs (1 matcher per policy), the per-policy cost is the same or slightly lower. For users opting into multi-matcher rules, cost scales with N matchers - this is an explicit opt-in cost and not a regression.

residual_risks: Production at 8-10k rps: the absolute numbers I gathered (1.5 us/req on the matcher path, 7 MB/s allocation) are well within budget for a fast machine, but the *direction* matters - this is pure overhead added to every request, every cache lookup, with a known and easy precompilation fix that the PR explicitly chose to defer. Aram's gating concern is legitimate.

Multi-matcher configs with many rules: O(N) Match + each rule's WildcardMatch is unbounded by current code. A user configuring 50+ rules per failsafe block will see linear blow-up. Not a current production concern (no such config exists in defaults) but a footgun without precompilation.

Concurrent Params read in Match (line 165): not strictly a perf issue, but flagged in finding perf-3 because the fix is unified. Without it, race detector will eventually fire on a load test that combines hedge/consensus retries with cache-policy matching.
testing_gaps: Zero benchmarks in matchers/ package - core ask of review #18 unmet.
No comparison benchmark (legacy getFailsafeExecutor vs new matcher-based getFailsafeExecutor) - cannot empirically claim parity or regression.

No load test at the 8-10k rps Aram stipulated; the ResetGock-based BenchmarkNetworkForward_* paths are useful for end-to-end perf but they don't isolate matcher cost from upstream/HTTP/parsing cost. An isolated matcher bench is needed to detect regressions independent of mock-server noise.

No allocation-budget assertion in CI (e.g., `b.ReportAllocs()` with a sentinel failing if Match() ever exceeds N allocs). Without this, future changes can quietly add allocations and the gating concern will resurface in prod.
verdict_for_pr_gating: DO NOT MERGE without addressing finding perf-4 (add benchmarks) at minimum. Perf-1 (precompilation) is strongly recommended given the explicit reviewer concern, but is acceptable to defer with a follow-up issue if the bench numbers from perf-4 land within the team's accepted budget at 10k rps. Perf-2 and perf-3 are trivial cleanups that should be folded in regardless.
